countries.name: Norway
countries.mechanism: Every child has a legal right to a safe school environment. Schools must act on any signal of bullying. The state can fine schools that fail.

countries.name: Sweden
countries.mechanism: Every school must publish an annual anti-bullying plan. The Schools Inspectorate audits compliance.
countries.punchline: Bullying prevention is a published plan, not a posters-on-a-wall promise.

countries.name: Japan
countries.law: Act for the Promotion of Measures to Prevent Bullying
countries.mechanism: Passed after a student's suicide. Mandates national policy + school-level prevention committees + investigation duty.
countries.punchline: It took a tragedy. India should not wait for one.

countries.name: South Korea
countries.law: School Violence Prevention and Countermeasures Act
countries.mechanism: School Violence Committees in every school. Findings can go on a bully's permanent school record — affecting university admissions.

countries.name: United Kingdom
countries.mechanism: Head teachers have a statutory duty to prevent bullying — including bullying that happens off-premises and online.
countries.punchline: Cyber is in scope. In India, it largely isn't.

countries.name: United States
countries.law: All 50 states have anti-bullying statutes; federal civil-rights law overlays.
countries.mechanism: State-level reporting, investigation, and discipline requirements. Federal action where bullying maps to protected-class harassment.

countries.name: Australia
countries.law: National Safe Schools Framework + state-level laws
countries.mechanism: Mandatory frameworks every school must adopt. Some states criminalise serious cyberbullying.

countries.name: India
countries.law: CBSE circulars + UGC Anti-Ragging Regulations (colleges only) + NCPCR guidelines
countries.mechanism: Advisory. Not binding on schools generally. Action depends on each school's willingness.
countries.punchline: A guideline. A school can follow it. Or not. Most don't.
